title: Paul Butler (bishop)

infobox.value: [Bishop of Southwell and Nottingham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Southwell_and_Nottingham "Bishop of Southwell and Nottingham") (2010–2014)  [Bishop of Southampton](/wiki/Bishop_of_Southampton "Bishop of Southampton") (2004–2009)

(born 18 September 1955) is a retired British [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") bishop and a former [Lord Spiritual](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual") of the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"). He was installed and enthroned in [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") on 22 February 2014 and was the [Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham"), the [diocesan bishop](/wiki/Diocesan_bishop "Diocesan bishop") of the [Diocese of Durham](/wiki/Diocese_of_Durham "Diocese of Durham"), from 20 January 2014 to 29 February 2024.[[4]](#cite_note-4) On 12 September 2013 it was announced that he had been appointed as bishop-designate of Durham (succeeding [Justin Welby](/wiki/Justin_Welby "Justin Welby").)[[5]](#cite_note-5) He was previously [bishop of Southwell and Nottingham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Southwell_and_Nottingham "Bishop of Southwell and Nottingham").[[6]](#cite_note-6) He was installed at Southwell Minster on 27 February 2010.[[7]](#cite_note-7) He served as the [suffragan](/wiki/Suffragan "Suffragan") [bishop of Southampton](/wiki/Bishop_of_Southampton "Bishop of Southampton") in the [Diocese of Winchester](/wiki/Diocese_of_Winchester "Diocese of Winchester") from 2004 until 2010.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Bishop of Durham from 2014 to 2024

**Paul Roger Butler** (born 18 September 1955) is a retired British [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") bishop and a former [Lord Spiritual](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual") of the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"). He was installed and enthroned in [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") on 22 February 2014 and was the [Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham"), the [diocesan bishop](/wiki/Diocesan_bishop "Diocesan bishop") of the [Diocese of Durham](/wiki/Diocese_of_Durham "Diocese of Durham"), from 20 January 2014 to 29 February 2024.[[4]](#cite_note-4) On 12 September 2013 it was announced that he had been appointed as bishop-designate of Durham (succeeding [Justin Welby](/wiki/Justin_Welby "Justin Welby").)[[5]](#cite_note-5) He was previously [bishop of Southwell and Nottingham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Southwell_and_Nottingham "Bishop of Southwell and Nottingham").[[6]](#cite_note-6) He was installed at Southwell Minster on 27 February 2010.[[7]](#cite_note-7) He served as the [suffragan](/wiki/Suffragan "Suffragan") [bishop of Southampton](/wiki/Bishop_of_Southampton "Bishop of Southampton") in the [Diocese of Winchester](/wiki/Diocese_of_Winchester "Diocese of Winchester") from 2004 until 2010.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Butler_(bishop)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Butler was educated at [Kingston Grammar School](/wiki/Kingston_Grammar_School "Kingston Grammar School") and received a BA in English and history with honours from the [University of Nottingham](/wiki/University_of_Nottingham "University of Nottingham") in 1977. He worked for the [Universities and Colleges Christian Fellowship](/wiki/Universities_and_Colleges_Christian_Fellowship "Universities and Colleges Christian Fellowship") (UCCF) in their Bookstall Service (1978–1980) before training for Ordained ministry at [Wycliffe Hall, Oxford](/wiki/Wycliffe_Hall,_Oxford "Wycliffe Hall, Oxford") from 1980 to 1983.

## Ordained ministry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Butler_(bishop)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ordained ministry")]

He was ordained a [deacon](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon") at [Petertide](/wiki/Petertide "Petertide") (on 3 July) 1983[[9]](#cite_note-9) and a [priest](/wiki/Priest "Priest") the following Petertide (1 July 1984), both times by Bishop [Ronald Bowlby](/wiki/Ronald_Bowlby "Ronald Bowlby") in [Southwark Cathedral](/wiki/Southwark_Cathedral "Southwark Cathedral").[[10]](#cite_note-10) He served his curacy between 1983 and 1987 at All Saints with Holy Trinity in Wandsworth in the [Diocese of Southwark](/wiki/Diocese_of_Southwark "Diocese of Southwark"). He then moved to [Scripture Union](/wiki/Scripture_Union "Scripture Union") to become Inner London Evangelist, holding this post until 1992. Between 1992 and 1994 he was Deputy Head of Mission at Scripture Union. Between 1987 and 1994, he was also a [non-stipendiary minister](/wiki/Non-stipendiary_minister "Non-stipendiary minister") at St Paul's East Ham in the [Diocese of Chelmsford](/wiki/Diocese_of_Chelmsford "Diocese of Chelmsford").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Butler then moved to the [Diocese of Chelmsford](/wiki/Diocese_of_Chelmsford "Diocese of Chelmsford"). Between 1994 and 1997 he was [priest-in-charge](/wiki/Priest-in-charge "Priest-in-charge") of [St. Mary's Church, Walthamstow](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Church,_Walthamstow "St. Mary's Church, Walthamstow") with St Stephen's and St Luke's, becoming team rector of [Walthamstow](/wiki/Walthamstow "Walthamstow") in 1997 until 2004. He was also area dean of [Waltham Forest](/wiki/Waltham_Forest "Waltham Forest") from 2000 to 2004 and was appointed an [honorary canon](/wiki/Honorary_canon "Honorary canon") of Byumba in [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda") in 2001.

### Episcopal ministry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Butler_(bishop)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Episcopal ministry")]

Butler was [consecrated](/wiki/Consecrated "Consecrated") by the [archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"), [Rowan Williams](/wiki/Rowan_Williams "Rowan Williams"), at [St Paul's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Paul%27s_Cathedral "St Paul's Cathedral") on 24 June 2004[[1]](#cite_note-cons-1) and then installed at [Winchester Cathedral](/wiki/Winchester_Cathedral "Winchester Cathedral") on 4 July 2004[[1]](#cite_note-cons-1) as the Bishop of Southampton. Butler also acts as an "Advocate for Children" amongst the bishops of the Church of England and as chairman of the Churches National Safeguarding Committee.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Butler is known for his annual "prayer-walks" in which he spends a week each year walking round a part of his diocese praying with local people.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In the 2018 [Lambeth Awards](/wiki/Lambeth_Awards "Lambeth Awards") given by the archbishop of Canterbury, Paul Butler received the [Hubert Walter](/wiki/Hubert_Walter "Hubert Walter") Award for Reconciliation and Interfaith Cooperation for service to the church in his role as lead bishop on safeguarding.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

He took part in the [2023 Coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_III_and_Camilla "Coronation of Charles III and Camilla") as one of the two Bishops Assistant to [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III"), alongside [Michael Beasley](/wiki/Michael_Beasley_(bishop) "Michael Beasley (bishop)"), [Bishop of Bath and Wells](/wiki/Bishop_of_Bath_and_Wells "Bishop of Bath and Wells").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

On 14 July 2023, he announced his retirement, effective 29 February 2024.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Views

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Butler_(bishop)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Views")]

Butler is concerned about [Universal Credit](/wiki/Universal_Credit "Universal Credit") and fears the two child limit will force children in families with more than two children into relative poverty. In 2018 Butler said, "If people fall out of work for whatever reason they will not get the protection if they've got three children now. The predictions from people like the independent financial services are that the child limit is actually going to have the biggest impact on increasing the number of children living in relative poverty. I would love the government to have the willingness to revisit that whole policy."[[19]](#cite_note-uc-19)

Butler is also concerned about monthly payments and about delays in paying benefit, he feels moving people from weekly payments to monthly payments is unrealistic.[[19]](#cite_note-uc-19)

Butler has stated that he would not conduct same-sex marriages as he continues to view marriage as a union between a man and a woman.[[20]](#cite_note-20) He voted against introducing "standalone services for same-sex couples" on a trial basis during a meeting of the General Synod in November 2023; the motion passed.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Nevertheless, in February 2023 he voted in favour of blessings for same-sex couples in the Church of England.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Personal life and other work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Butler_(bishop)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Personal life and other work")]

He was a trustee of the [Church Mission Society](/wiki/Church_Mission_Society "Church Mission Society") from 2000 to 2010 and its chairman from 2008 to 2010. He regularly visits Rwanda, Burundi and Uganda,[[24]](#cite_note-24) and visited Natal, South Africa in January 2011 as part of a diocesan visit.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

He has also served the YMCA at Forest (1995–2004), Southampton (2004–09), Nottingham (2010–13), Teesdale (2014-present).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2012 he was appointed president of Scripture Union, the charity he had worked for from 1987 to 1994.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

He is a patron of the Scargill Movement (which operates [Scargill House](/wiki/Scargill_House "Scargill House"))[[27]](#cite_note-27) as well as the Nottingham-based charity the Ear Foundation.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In March 2015, it was revealed that Butler had, a year previously, reported former Conservative MP [Enoch Powell](/wiki/Enoch_Powell "Enoch Powell") as being involved with a Westminster paedophile ring in the 1980s, after the allegations were passed to him by [Dominic Walker](/wiki/Dominic_Walker_(bishop) "Dominic Walker (bishop)"), the former bishop of Monmouth.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Safeguarding controversy and the Elliott Review

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Butler_(bishop)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Safeguarding controversy and the Elliott Review")]

Butler was the bishop at the centre of the Elliott Review which led to damning headlines across UK media in March 2016.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) The review ushered in major change to the church's response to survivors of church sex abuse, and condemned as "reckless" the way in which Butler cut contact with a survivor following advice from insurers to limit liability. The highly critical report concluded there had been a string of "deeply disturbing" failures by senior Church of England figures, including the office of the archbishop of Canterbury, [Justin Welby](/wiki/Justin_Welby "Justin Welby"). It highlighted the need for all bishops to be urgently retrained.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) The church described the review "embarrassing and uncomfortable".[[38]](#cite_note-38) The survivor called Butler's response "inadequate"[[39]](#cite_note-39) and in a public letter[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) urged him to lead a call for [repentance](/wiki/Repentance "Repentance") across the [House of Bishops](/wiki/House_of_Bishops "House of Bishops").

## Publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Butler_(bishop)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Publications")]

He has a number of publications, including *Reaching Children* (1992), *Reaching Families* (1995) (both Scripture Union); *Temptation & Testing* (SPCK, 2007) and is a contributor to *Through the Eyes of a Child* (Church House 2009). His most recent publication is *Living Your Confirmation* (SPCK, 2012).

The Church of England. 13 November 2023. Retrieved 23 November 2023. "7. The motion (as amended) 'That this Synod, conscious that the Church is not of one mind on the issues raised by Living in Love and Faith, that we are in a period of uncertainty, and that many in the Church on all sides are being deeply hurt at this time, recognise the progress made by the House of Bishops towards implementing the motion on Living in Love and Faith passed by this Synod in February 2023, as reported in GS 2328, encourage the House to continue its work of implementation, and ask the House to consider whether some standalone services for same-sex couples could be made available for use, possibly on a trial basis, on the timescale envisaged by the motion passed by the Synod in February 2023.' was carried following a counted vote by Houses."
